title: Robot Arm Project II
subtitle: Smarter Arm, Sharper Code — Level Up!
overview: Building on the Robot Arm Project (Session 13), this session upgrades the arm with smarter control — adding sensor-driven automation, inverse kinematics concepts, serial command input, and obstacle detection. Students evolve their manual arm into a semi-autonomous robot that can react to its environment.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — serial monitor command input, ultrasonic sensor mounted on gripper, inverse kinematics diagram, PCA9685 servo driver board, smooth sweep code output.

keyTopics: Smooth Servo Movement — Incrementally stepping servo angles with small delays for fluid, professional motion
Serial Command Control — Sending text commands via Serial Monitor to control individual joints in real time
Sensor-Driven Automation — Mounting an ultrasonic sensor on the gripper for obstacle detection and auto-stop
Inverse Kinematics (Concept) — Given a target XY position, calculate the required joint angles to reach it
PCA9685 Servo Driver — I2C servo driver board that controls up to 16 servos using only 2 Arduino pins
EEPROM Storage — Saving recorded arm positions to non-volatile memory so sequences survive power-off
Gripper Feedback — Using a force/flex sensor or current sensing to detect when the gripper has gripped an object
Autonomous Pick and Place — Combining sensor input, logic, and sequencing for a fully automated arm routine

activity.title: Semi-Autonomous Robot Arm — Serial Control + Obstacle Detection + Sequence Memory
parts: Robot Arm from Session 13 (assembled with 4 servos)
USB cable + laptop with Arduino IDE
Small objects for pick and place testing
steps: Part 1 — Smooth Movement: Replace all servo.write(angle) calls with a smoothMove() function that steps the servo from current angle to target angle in increments of 2° with a 15ms delay between steps
Test: observe how the arm now moves fluidly instead of snapping to position
Part 2 — Serial Command Control: In loop(), read Serial input — parse commands like 'B90' (Base to 90°), 'S45' (Shoulder to 45°), 'G0' (Gripper open), 'G180' (Gripper close)
Upload and open Serial Monitor at 9600 baud — type commands and watch the corresponding joint respond
Part 3 — Obstacle Detection: Mount HC-SR04 on the gripper facing forward
Add distance checking before every movement — if distance < 5cm, stop all servos and print 'OBSTACLE DETECTED' to Serial Monitor
Test: move a hand in front of the sensor mid-movement — arm should pause automatically
Part 4 — Sequence Memory: Record 6 joint-angle snapshots into int positions[6][4] arrays during a manual run
Add a playback mode triggered by typing 'PLAY' in Serial Monitor — arm replays the full sequence automatically
Challenge: Save the sequence to EEPROM using EEPROM.write() so the arm remembers the routine after power-off and auto-plays on startup!

quiz.q: Why is SMOOTH servo movement better than single-jump commands for a robot arm?
options: Smooth movement uses less power
Smooth movement looks more professional, reduces mechanical stress on joints, and gives more precise and controllable motion
Single-jump commands are not supported by the Servo library
Smooth movement is faster than single-jump commands
quiz.explanation: Smooth = professional and safe! Large single jumps stress the servo gears and make the arm unpredictable. Small incremental steps (2° at a time with short delays) produce fluid motion — just like real industrial robot arms move!

quiz.q: How do you make a servo move SMOOTHLY from 30° to 90° in Arduino?
options: servo.write(90) — the servo automatically moves smoothly
Use a for loop: for(int angle = 30; angle <= 90; angle++) { servo.write(angle); delay(15); }
Call servo.smooth(30, 90) from the Servo library
Use analogWrite() instead of servo.write() for smooth motion
quiz.explanation: Smooth sweep = for loop with small delay! Step from 30° to 90° one degree at a time, pausing 15ms between each step. The servo physically moves to each intermediate position — creating smooth, controlled fluid motion!

quiz.q: What is SERIAL COMMAND CONTROL of a robot arm?
options: Controlling the arm using only potentiometers
Sending text commands through the Serial Monitor (e.g. 'B90', 'G0') that the Arduino parses and translates into servo movements
Uploading a new program to change the arm's behaviour
Connecting the arm to a smartphone via Bluetooth
quiz.explanation: Serial control = text-based remote control! You type a command like 'S45' (Shoulder to 45°). Arduino reads the serial input, extracts the joint letter and angle number, and moves the correct servo. Powerful and flexible — no extra hardware needed!

quiz.q: What does Serial.parseInt() do in Arduino?
options: Converts a float to an integer
Reads incoming serial data and extracts the next integer value from the stream
Prints an integer to the Serial Monitor
Checks if an integer has been received on the serial port
quiz.explanation: Serial.parseInt() = number extractor! When the user types 'B90', Serial.read() gets 'B', then Serial.parseInt() extracts 90 as an integer. Perfect for parsing angle commands from the Serial Monitor without complex string parsing!

quiz.q: What is INVERSE KINEMATICS (IK) in robotics?
options: Moving all joints in the opposite direction simultaneously
Calculating the forward speed of the end-effector
Given a desired position (X, Y, Z) of the gripper in space, calculating the joint angles required to reach that position
The process of reversing a recorded movement sequence
quiz.explanation: IK = 'I want my hand HERE — what angles do my joints need?' The opposite of Forward Kinematics. IK is mathematically complex (trigonometry) but is how all modern robot arms work — you specify the target, the computer calculates the joint angles!

quiz.q: What is the PCA9685 board used for?
options: A motor driver for DC motors only
A 16-channel PWM servo driver that connects to Arduino via I2C — allowing control of up to 16 servos using only 2 Arduino pins
A sensor board for measuring distance and temperature
A Bluetooth module for wireless servo control
quiz.explanation: PCA9685 = servo expander! Arduino UNO only has 6 PWM pins — limiting you to 6 servos. PCA9685 adds 16 PWM channels via I2C (just pins SDA and SCL). Perfect for complex arms with many joints — one board, 16 servos, 2 wires!

options: A wireless protocol for sending data between two Arduinos
A two-wire serial communication protocol (SDA + SCL) that allows multiple devices to communicate with a microcontroller on the same bus
A type of PWM signal used for precise servo control
A high-speed USB protocol for uploading code to Arduino
quiz.explanation: I2C = two-wire bus! SDA (data) + SCL (clock). Multiple devices can share the same two wires — each gets a unique address. PCA9685 uses I2C. So do many sensors (OLED displays, IMUs, temperature sensors). Very efficient use of Arduino pins!

options: A type of RAM that stores data only while the Arduino is powered on
Non-volatile memory built into the Arduino chip — data written here survives power-off and is still there when the Arduino restarts
External SD card storage for large datasets
A special type of servo controller memory
quiz.explanation: EEPROM = permanent memory! Unlike RAM (lost on power-off), EEPROM keeps data when unplugged. Arduino UNO has 1KB of EEPROM. Write servo positions with EEPROM.write(), read them back with EEPROM.read() — arm remembers its routine forever!

quiz.q: How much EEPROM storage does an Arduino UNO have?
options: 4KB
512 bytes
1KB (1024 bytes)
32KB
quiz.explanation: Arduino UNO = 1024 bytes (1KB) of EEPROM! Each byte stores one value (0–255). To store a servo angle (0–180), one byte per joint is enough. 4 joints × 6 positions = 24 bytes — well within the 1KB limit for a sequence memory system!

quiz.q: What is OBSTACLE DETECTION in the context of a robot arm?
options: Detecting when the arm has dropped an object
Using a sensor (e.g. ultrasonic) mounted on the arm to detect objects in its path and automatically pause or stop movement to prevent collisions
A safety switch that cuts power if the arm exceeds its angle limits
Software that prevents the arm from moving beyond 180°
quiz.explanation: Obstacle detection = collision avoidance! Mount HC-SR04 on the gripper. Before each movement, check distance. If distance < safe threshold → halt all servos and alert. Prevents the arm from smashing into objects — essential in real industrial arms!

quiz.q: What does millis() help achieve in servo control compared to delay()?
options: millis() makes servos move faster than delay()
millis() allows non-blocking timing — the Arduino can check sensors and respond to commands WHILE servos are moving, unlike delay() which freezes everything
millis() provides more precise PWM signals to servos
millis() reduces the power consumption of servo motors
quiz.explanation: millis() = multitasking! delay() freezes Arduino — during a 500ms delay, NO sensors are read, NO serial commands are processed. millis() lets you track time without blocking. The arm can move AND listen for commands AND read sensors simultaneously!

quiz.q: What is GRIPPER FEEDBACK and why is it useful?
options: A camera that shows the operator what the gripper sees
Using a force sensor, flex sensor, or current monitoring to detect when the gripper has made contact with or successfully gripped an object
The sound the gripper servo makes when it reaches its limit
A signal sent back to the Serial Monitor showing current gripper angle
quiz.explanation: Gripper feedback = sense of touch! Without it, the arm blindly closes the gripper and hopes for the best. With a force sensor, it closes until resistance is detected — then stops. Prevents crushing delicate objects and confirms successful grip!

quiz.q: What is a SEQUENCE ARRAY in a robot arm program?
options: A list of all components used in the arm
A 2D array storing sets of joint angles for each step of a recorded movement — replayed in order to automate a task
An array of PWM frequencies for each servo motor
A list of Serial commands received during a session
quiz.explanation: Sequence array = robot's memory of movements! int positions[6][4] stores 6 snapshots, each with 4 joint angles. Loop through it with for(step) and smoothMove() each servo to its stored angle. The arm perfectly repeats a task automatically — every time!

quiz.q: What real-world robot uses inverse kinematics and sensor feedback together?
options: A simple doorbell
A weather vane
A surgical robot like the Da Vinci system — IK calculates tool positions while force sensors provide haptic feedback to the surgeon
A standard home thermostat
quiz.explanation: Da Vinci surgical robot = IK + sensor feedback at the highest level! Surgeons control it remotely — IK translates hand movements into precise instrument positions inside the patient. Force sensors provide feedback. Saves lives with millimetre precision!

quiz.q: What is the main advantage of AUTONOMOUS pick and place over MANUAL potentiometer control?
options: Autonomous control is simpler to program
Manual control is slower and less accurate — autonomous control executes the same precise movement repeatedly at consistent speed without human error
Autonomous control does not require any sensors
Manual control requires more servo motors than autonomous control
quiz.explanation: Automation = consistency and speed! A human turning potentiometers varies every time — different speed, slightly different angles. Autonomous sequence playback hits the exact same angles at the exact same speed every single cycle — that is why factories use robots!
